summary: I was working in the flower garden at Arlington House on the morning of September 11, 2001. It was a beautiful day, and I was trimming plants when I first heard about the plane hitting one of the towers in New York City. Arlington House was where I was stationed, and it was from there that I witnessed the unfolding events and later had to evacuate the mountain. The place became a vantage point as the FBI set up a command station nearby to oversee the situation.

New York City was at the center of the tragedy on September 11, 2001, when two planes hit the Twin Towers. I learned about it while working at Arlington House, first hearing on the radio about a plane hitting one tower, then realizing two planes had struck. The news was confirmed by a New York City cop who was visiting with his family there. The collapse of the towers was something I heard about on the radio and saw later on TV, which added to the chaos and fear that day.

The World Trade Center was struck by two planes on the morning of September 11, 2001. I first heard about the attack while at Arlington House, and it was shocking to learn that both towers had been hit. The news came from a New York City police officer who was there with his family. Later, I watched on TV as the towers collapsed, which was a haunting and unforgettable sight.

On September 11, 2001, I was working at Arlington House when I first heard about the attacks. It started with news of a plane hitting one of the Twin Towers in New York City, then a second plane hit, and soon after, the Pentagon was struck. The day was filled with confusion and fear as bridges were closed and people evacuated. I experienced the chaos firsthand while trying to get home, eventually leaving my truck and walking due to traffic. The attacks deeply affected me, and I spent hours watching the events unfold on TV that evening.

I saw the Pentagon burning on September 11, 2001, shortly after the plane hit it. From Arlington House, I walked to the end of the garden and saw smoke pouring out, though flames were hard to see. It was surreal because the Pentagon is such a central place for the country’s leadership. Later, I learned the Pentagon was being evacuated, adding to the mass confusion in the area. The sight of the burning Pentagon made the attacks feel even more real and terrifying.

On the day of the attacks, I tried to get home by driving over Memorial Bridge, which would have taken me directly into D.C. However, the bridge was closed as part of the security measures. This forced me to find alternate routes and contributed to the heavy traffic and confusion that day. The closure of Memorial Bridge was one of several obstacles that made leaving the area very difficult.

After Memorial Bridge was closed on September 11, 2001, I tried to use the next bridge, which I believe was the Key Bridge, but it was also shut down. This meant I had to keep following the river and find other ways to get home. The closing of Key Bridge was part of the widespread effort to control movement in and out of D.C. and added to the chaos and gridlock that day.

I live near the District of Columbia, and on September 11, 2001, I was trying to get into D.C. after work but faced massive traffic and bridge closures. People were evacuating and walking everywhere, creating a tense and confusing atmosphere. The city was on high alert due to the attacks, and many were desperate to leave or get home safely. Eventually, I parked my truck and walked into D.C., reflecting on the gravity of what was happening.

On September 11, 2001, I heard that the Federal Bureau of Investigation was setting up a command station at Arlington House to oversee the situation in the city. This was part of the response to the attacks, and it contributed to the decision to evacuate the area. The FBI’s presence indicated the seriousness of the events and the need for coordinated security efforts.

After the attacks on September 11, 2001, I learned from a friend who called the White House that the United States Park Service was going to have us back to work the next day despite everything. The White House was a central point of communication during that chaotic time, and it was reassuring to hear that some normalcy was expected soon. It showed how quickly the government was trying to respond and maintain order.

I was working for the United States Park Service at Arlington House on September 11, 2001. After the attacks, there was confusion and evacuation, but the next day we were told to return to work. A friend called the White House to confirm that the Park Service would be operating as usual. Despite the tragedy, the Park Service continued its duties, and I went back to work, standing in the back and processing the events that had unfolded.
